content.text: What It Is

An Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) for enterprise AI systems is the governance document that defines what uses of AI tools are permitted, what uses are subject to additional controls or approval, and what uses are prohibited for employees and contractors of the organisation. It translates the organisation's AI governance principles and risk appetite into operational rules that individuals can understand and follow in their daily use of AI tools. Unlike high-level AI strategy documents or ethics principles that operate at an abstract level, the AUP contains specific, actionable guidance on the types of AI use that are and are not acceptable.

The scope of an enterprise AI AUP must cover three dimensions: the people it applies to (employees, contractors, agency workers, and potentially third parties accessing the organisation's AI-enabled systems), the AI systems it covers (which tools and platforms are in scope — all organisation-procured AI tools, personal-use AI tools accessed via personal devices for work purposes, custom-built AI systems, and third-party AI integrated into the organisation's products), and the use contexts it addresses (what activities using AI are addressed by the policy, from internal productivity use through to customer-facing applications and AI-assisted professional advice).

The prohibited uses section of the AUP is its most consequential component. It must be specific enough to provide clear guidance on what is not acceptable, without being so detailed that it fails to capture novel uses that were not anticipated when the policy was written. The best practice approach uses a combination of specific prohibitions (these exact categories of use are prohibited) and principle-based prohibitions (any use that has these characteristics is prohibited, even if not explicitly listed). This dual structure allows the policy to be clear on known risks while retaining the flexibility to address new AI capabilities as they emerge.
Why It Matters

Without an AUP, AI use in the enterprise defaults to the individual judgment of each employee — producing inconsistent, undocumented, and often inappropriate AI use that the organisation has no visibility into and no mechanism to govern. A researcher who does not know the policy on using AI to assist with competitive intelligence analysis may unknowingly upload confidential competitor information to a consumer AI service. A lawyer who is not told that AI-generated legal analysis requires human review may submit AI outputs directly to clients without verification. A marketer who is not given guidance on AI-generated content may produce materials that make unfounded claims. In each case, the harm arises not from malicious intent but from the absence of governance.

The AUP also provides the framework for disciplinary enforcement. Without a published, trained AUP, the organisation cannot take disciplinary action against an employee who misuses AI in a way that causes harm — because the employee can legitimately claim they were not informed that the use was prohibited. The AUP, properly communicated and acknowledged, establishes the baseline against which employee conduct can be measured and, where necessary, disciplinary action can be taken.
Benefits
Use consistency:
A well-designed AUP ensures that AI use across the organisation follows consistent standards rather than individual judgment.
Risk visibility:
AUP compliance monitoring provides visibility into AI use patterns that would otherwise be invisible to governance functions.
Disciplinary framework:
A communicated and acknowledged AUP provides the legal basis for disciplinary action when AI is misused.
Data protection compliance:
AUP rules on personal data input into AI systems support GDPR and equivalent compliance.
IP protection:
AUP rules on what confidential information can be shared with AI systems protect the organisation's intellectual property.
Trust building:
A clear, fair AUP that explains the rationale for restrictions builds employee trust in the organisation's AI governance.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Over-restriction risk:
An AUP that prohibits too broadly will be ignored or circumvented; restrictions must be proportionate to the risk.
Shadow AI:
Employees who find the AUP too restrictive will use personal devices and consumer AI tools not subject to the policy; overly restrictive AUPs drive AI use underground rather than governing it.
Pace of AI development:
New AI capabilities emerge faster than AUP update cycles; the policy must include a principle-based catch-all for novel uses not explicitly addressed.
Enforcement inconsistency:
An AUP that is not consistently enforced loses its deterrent effect; enforcement must be systematic and fair.
Prohibited Use Categories

Enterprise AI AUPs typically define prohibited uses across six categories. First, personal data processing without appropriate legal basis: using AI tools to process personal data about individuals — customers, employees, third parties — in ways that lack the required legal basis under GDPR, CCPA, or equivalent data protection law. This includes uploading datasets containing personal information to consumer AI services that do not have appropriate data processing agreements, using AI to profile individuals in ways that require explicit consent not obtained, and processing sensitive categories of personal data (health, financial, biometric) in AI tools not approved for that data classification.

Second, confidential information to unauthorised AI systems: sharing the organisation's confidential business information, client information, trade secrets, or strategic plans with AI services not approved to receive that classification of information. Many consumer and developer AI services process and potentially train on user inputs; uploading confidential information to these services creates IP leakage risk that may not be recoverable.

Third, AI-assisted deception: using AI to create false, misleading, or deceptive content — fabricated evidence, synthetic identities, deepfake materials, false customer reviews — in any context. This category should also include AI-generated phishing or social engineering content, regardless of the stated purpose.

Fourth, discriminatory or biased AI deployment: using AI systems in decision-making about individuals in ways that produce or perpetuate illegal discrimination. This covers both the deliberate use of AI to discriminate and the inadvertent deployment of AI systems without adequate bias testing and monitoring.

Fifth, unlicensed or unapproved AI deployment: using AI tools that have not been assessed and approved through the organisation's AI procurement or deployment process. Shadow IT AI is a significant risk category; the AUP must clearly require that all AI tools used for work purposes are either on the approved list or submitted for assessment before use.

Sixth, professional advice without required human review: using AI-generated content as the final output for professional advice — legal, financial, medical, engineering — without the human expert review that the professional standards of the relevant profession require. AI-assisted draft is permitted; AI-final without review is prohibited.
Architecture

attrs.alt: AUP scope framework: define scope → categorise uses → specify controls → document exceptions → publish and train → review and update

content.text: The six-stage AUP development and maintenance cycle ensures the policy remains current and operationally effective. The use categorisation stage is the most important: each AI use case the organisation encounters should be assessed against three categories: permitted (use is appropriate within general guidelines), restricted (use requires additional controls or approval), and prohibited (use is not acceptable regardless of controls). This three-way categorisation is more operationally useful than a binary permitted/prohibited framework because it provides a middle ground for nuanced use cases.
How to Implement
Define scope precisely: list the AI systems in scope by category (organisation-provided tools; contractor-provided tools; personal AI tools used for work); list the individuals and roles in scope.

Draft the use categorisation matrix: for each major AI use context, define whether the use is permitted, restricted (with what controls), or prohibited (with the rationale); circulate for review with Legal, HR, IT, and representative business unit leads.

Draft specific prohibited use rules using both specific examples and principle-based catch-alls: 'including but not limited to' lists with a principle-based extension prevent the policy from being gamed by novel use cases not explicitly listed.

Define the exception approval process: for uses that fall in restricted or ambiguous categories, define who can approve an exception, what information they need to make the decision, and how exceptions are documented.

Consult with employee representatives (works councils, trade unions) before finalising: AUPs covering employee conduct require consultation in co-determined jurisdictions and best-practice engagement in others.
Integrate AUP acknowledgement into the onboarding process and annual training cycle; maintain attestation records for disciplinary and regulatory purposes.
Schedule quarterly AUP review to address new AI capabilities and use cases; assign a named owner responsible for maintaining the policy.
Examples

# AUP exception request tracking

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal, Optional

ExceptionStatus = Literal["submitted","under_review","approved","rejected","expired"]

@dataclass
class AupExceptionRequest:
    request_id: str
    requestor_employee_id: str
    business_unit: str
    ai_use_description: str
    aup_rule_reference: str       # which AUP clause the exception applies to
    business_justification: str
    proposed_compensating_controls: list[str]
    submission_date: date
    expiry_date: Optional[date]   # exceptions are time-limited
    reviewed_by: Optional[str] = None
    status: ExceptionStatus = "submitted"
    rejection_reason: str = ""

    def is_expired(self) -> bool:
        return self.expiry_date is not None and date.today() > self.expiry_date and self.status == "approved"

def exception_summary(requests: list[AupExceptionRequest]) -> dict:
    approved = [r for r in requests if r.status == "approved"]
    expired = [r for r in approved if r.is_expired()]
    return {
        "total_requests": len(requests),
        "approved": len(approved),
        "rejected": sum(1 for r in requests if r.status == "rejected"),
        "pending": sum(1 for r in requests if r.status in ("submitted","under_review")),
        "active_exceptions": len(approved) - len(expired),
        "expired_exceptions": len(expired),
        "most_common_rule": max(
            set(r.aup_rule_reference for r in requests),
            key=lambda ref: sum(1 for r in requests if r.aup_rule_reference == ref),
            default=None
        ),
    }
Real-World Example

A professional services firm published its first AI AUP in early 2024 following several incidents: a consultant had uploaded a client's draft merger agreement to a consumer AI tool for summarisation, creating a potential client confidentiality breach; a junior analyst had submitted an AI-generated financial model directly to a client without verification, which contained a calculation error; and a team had used a personal-account AI image generator to create marketing materials, inadvertently using an image style that was the subject of an active copyright dispute.

The AUP addressed each incident type with specific rules: all client information was classified as confidential and prohibited from input into any AI system not on the firm's approved AI tool list; all AI-generated analysis intended for client delivery required named senior professional sign-off before transmission; and all AI-generated images for commercial use required Legal review of the generation tool's IP terms before use. The policy also included a principle-based catch-all: 'any use of AI that the employee would not be comfortable disclosing to their client, their manager, or the firm's general counsel is presumptively restricted and requires pre-approval.' The firm found this heuristic useful for addressing novel AI use cases not explicitly covered by specific rules.
Common Mistakes
Binary permitted/prohibited without restricted category:
A two-tier policy leaves no room for nuanced guidance on uses that require controls rather than prohibition.
No catch-all principle:
Specific-only prohibitions are gamed by novel use cases; include a principle-based catch-all.
No exception process:
Without an exception process, employees facing legitimate but non-standard use cases either violate the policy or forgo a useful capability.
No employee engagement:
An AUP written entirely by Legal and IT without employee input produces rules that are impractical to follow and resented by users.
Irregular review:
An AUP that is not reviewed regularly becomes misaligned with actual AI capabilities and use patterns within 6-12 months.
Best Practices
Write the AUP in plain language that non-technical employees can understand; a policy that requires a lawyer to interpret will not be followed consistently.
Use real examples from within the organisation (anonymised) to illustrate prohibited uses; concrete examples are more effective than abstract principles.
Include positive guidance (what employees should do) alongside restrictions (what they must not do); policy documents that are entirely prohibitive fail to channel AI use toward good practice.
Review and update the AUP at least quarterly in the current pace of AI development; assign a named policy owner accountable for maintaining currency.
Publish the AUP on the intranet with version control; employees should be able to find the current version and see what changed in each version.
Data Classification and AI Tool Matching

One of the most operationally useful components of a well-designed AUP is an explicit mapping between the organisation's data classification framework and the AI tools approved to process each data classification level. Most organisations already operate a data classification framework (typically: public, internal, confidential, restricted/secret); the AUP extends this framework to AI tool selection by specifying which approved AI tools are authorised to process which data classifications.

This data-AI tool mapping prevents the most common category of AUP violation: an employee using an AI tool that is genuinely on the approved list but using it with data at a higher classification level than the tool is authorised to process. For example, an organisation may approve a consumer AI writing assistant for use with public and internal data, an enterprise AI platform with appropriate data processing agreements for confidential data, and no AI tool at all for restricted data without explicit DPO and CISO sign-off. Making this mapping explicit removes the ambiguity that leads to inadvertent misuse.
Public data:
Any approved AI tool; no additional restrictions on use context.
Internal data:
Approved enterprise AI tools only; consumer AI tools with no DPA prohibited; AI outputs containing internal data not to be shared externally without review.
Confidential data:
Enterprise AI tools with executed DPA and data residency confirmation only; AI processing log required; DPO notification for new use cases.
Restricted data:
No AI tool processing without named CISO and DPO approval; approved tools must have air-gapped or on-premises deployment; all processing logged and audited.
Special category personal data (GDPR):
Restricted-level controls apply regardless of general data classification; DPIA required before AI processing.
AUP Governance for Agentic AI

Agentic AI systems that can take autonomous real-world actions present AUP governance challenges that standard AI use policies were not designed to address. An individual employee deciding to ask an AI chatbot a question exercises personal judgment about the appropriateness of that use; an AI agent that is authorised to send emails, place orders, or modify records on behalf of the organisation takes actions that the AUP framework must govern differently — not at the point of individual use, but at the point of agent deployment and scope authorisation.

AUP provisions for agentic AI must address four additional dimensions. First, agent scope authorisation: who is authorised to define what actions an AI agent can take autonomously, and what approval is required before a new action type is added to an agent's scope? Second, data access governance: what data can an agent access autonomously, and what data access requires explicit human authorisation for each access event? Third, action reversibility requirements: are there categories of agent actions that must always be reversible, or that trigger mandatory human review before execution? Fourth, agent identity and accountability: when an AI agent takes an action, which human principal is accountable for that action, and how is this documented in the organisation's records?
Key Takeaways
The AUP translates abstract AI governance principles into specific operational rules; without it, AI use defaults to individual judgment with predictably inconsistent outcomes.
A three-tier categorisation (permitted / restricted / prohibited) is more operationally useful than binary permitted/prohibited.
Principle-based catch-alls prevent the policy from being gamed by novel use cases not explicitly anticipated when the policy was written.
An exception process is essential; without one, employees in legitimate but non-standard situations either violate the policy or forgo a useful capability.
The AUP must be reviewed at least quarterly; AI capabilities and use patterns evolve faster than annual policy cycles can track.
Employee engagement in AUP development — through consultation, pilot feedback, or works council review — produces policies that are both more practically workable and more likely to be followed.
